data.title: Bristol-Myers Q2 Earnings Review: Solid Quarter, But Incoming CMO Must Fix Pipeline
data.description:
Investment Overview

Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (NYSE:BMY) reported its Q2 2025 earnings yesterday. The Princeton, New Jersey-based Pharma giant exceeded the Street's expectations, reporting $12.3bn of revenues, a figure that was up ~1% year-on-year, and normalized earnings per share ("EPS") of $1.46, although GAAP EPS of $0.64 - which includes $(0.57) of in-process research and development ("IPR&D"), fell marginally short of analysts' forecasts.

BMY also raised its revenue guidance for the full-year 2025, from $45.8 - $46.8bn of revenues, to $46.5bn $47.5bn of revenues, and lowered its non-GAAP EPS assumptions $6.7 - $7, to $6.35 - $6.65.

In my last note for Seeking Alpha on BMY, covering Q1 earnings in late April, I gave shares a Hold rating, with shares priced at $47 per share, although stock has in fact declined by 11% since, trading at $44 pre-market today, representing a market cap of $89.6bn. That is down >25% on a 6-month basis, and also down 25% on a five-year basis, and down 23% year-to-date.

I described BMY as a "two-tier" company, with a "growth portfolio," and a "legacy portfolio," which is illustrated neatly in the following table:

In the first table, we can see the "growth products," and in the lower table, the "legacy portfolio," and I have shaded red where patent expiries have already taken place, placing revenues in permanent decline as a result of generic drug competition.

After its $74bn acquisition of Celgene back in 2019, BMY's annual revenues leaped from $26.1bn in that year, to $42.5bn in 2020, and then $46.4bn in 2021, then $46.2bn, $45bn, and $48.3bn, in 2022, 2023, and last year in 2024.

BMY's long-term goal has always been to offset revenues of patent expired products with new drug launches, and although 2025 will be a "trough year," i.e., revenues will fall compared to 2024, we can see that in Q225, revenues from the growth product portfolio of $6.6bn accounted for 54% of total revenues, whereas in FY24, they had accounted for 47%.

With that said, BMY does have an ongoing reliance on two assets - its solid tumor cancer med Opdivo, and its anticoagulant Eliquis, which accounted for >50% of revenues in Q2—both of which face patent expiries in the coming years—Eliquis potentially as early as next year, and Opdivo in 2028.

Opdualog is well on its way to becoming a "blockbuster" (>$1bn revenues per annum) drug, with a 30% share of the first line melanoma market, management says, however recent study data of Opdualog in combo with a subcutaneously administered form of Opdivo (nivolumab) came up short, which is notable, as an approval for subQ Opdivo may potentially extend that drug's patent protections.

Meanwhile, Cobenfy, BMY's newly launched schizophrenia drug, acquired via its $12bn acquisition of Karuna Therapeutics, has gotten off to a reasonable start commercially, however the drug failed to meet endpoints in a key study in patients failing to respond to their current treatment. Analysts had viewed Cobenfy as a potential double-digit billion-dollar selling asset, but have since revised projections downwards.

As such, there is arguably some pressure building on BMY to show how valuable its current and future assets can be, to arrest a slightly concerning share price slump, the only positive being that its dividend payout of $0.62 per quarter now translates to an annual yield of nearly 6%

BMY's "Data Rich" Period Is Almost Upon Us—Can It Succeed?

BMY has (so far as I am aware) not provided any specific long-term revenue guidance, but management assures us the company is entering an exciting period, with a large number of key data catalysts approaching, as per the slide below, from its Q2 earnings presentation:

Iberdomide is a drug targeting a market BMY knows well - multiple myeloma - thanks to the former success of pomalyst and revlimid, the former now patent expired, and the latter set to lose protection after 2026—hence its data readout later this year—and, perhaps more importantly, Progression Free Survival data due early next year, are key to setting up long-term control of a crucial market.

BMY has talked up its cereblon E3 ligase modulatory drugs ("CELMoDs") platform in MM, which includes mezigdomide as well as iberdomide, as a potential new standard of care ("SoC") - soon we will discover if this is truly the case, or if BMY is in danger of losing ground to, e.g., Johnson & Johnson' (JNJ) and Legend Biotech Corporation's (LEGN) Carvykti - $963m of revenues in 2024; JNJ's Talvey - $500m; Darzalex - $11.7bn; and Amgen Inc.'s (AMGN) Kyprolis - $1.7bn revenues in 2024.

Milvexian is a potential next-generation blood-thinner and successor to Eliquis, which analysts have formerly suggested could achieve >$3.5bn in peak revenues, however on yesterday's earnings call, one analyst asked why that figure had fallen to ~$2.3bn, reflecting "a lot of skepticism." Key data falls due early next year.

It's also important to BMY's growth plans that Cobenfy can show its mettle in studies in Alzheimer's Agitation, a potential multi-billion dollar market, and in bi-polar, also, while, looking at earlier stage assets, the jury remains out on Krazati in non-small cell lung cancer ("NSCLC"), whether a CD19 targeting cell therapy can translate impressive performance in oncology into an autoimmune condition, and whether the mid-stage promise shown by BMY's PRMT5 inhibitor will be reproduced in a Phase 2/3 study.

Amidst all of these data catalysts and "ifs, buts and maybes," it's also worth noting that BMY announced last week the departure of its Chief Medical Officer Samit Hirawat, who is set to be replaced by Cristian Massacesi, formerly Chief Medical Officer at AstraZeneca PLC (AZN), the Anglo-Swedish Pharma giant. This can be interpreted either as worrying news—Hirawat is paying the price for recent study misses, or as a positive, as BMY CEO Chris Boerner suggested on yesterday's earnings call:

I think in many respects, not just with respect to the early pipeline, but really across drug development, there's going to be a focus that we have on execution. As we've talked about, our pipeline and delivering on that pipeline is very important. And so we have to be acutely focused on strong execution. Cristian has a very strong background in that respect across his career, particularly with respect to his last company. Also, when you think about executing at scale, ensuring that you have the strongest, highest-performing teams with the best people that you can find is going to be important. And again, he has a very strong track record there.

Analysis - BMY's Business Is Robust On Many Levels, But Success Is A Must For Incoming CMO

On many levels, BMY remains an investable blue-chip Pharma giant in my view. BMY earned the eighth largest amount of revenues of any global Pharma in 2024, ahead of the likes of Eli Lilly and Company (LLY) - market cap >$730bn, Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO), market cap $229bn, Amgen Inc. (AMGN), market cap $158bn, and Gilead Sciences, Inc. (GILD), market cap $135bn.

While Lilly and Novo are in possession of "miracle" GLP-1 weight loss drugs in tirzepatide and semaglutide respectively, and Amgen is developing one in MariTide, which helps to explain their higher valuations, in the hear and now, BMY's portfolio and pipeline stands up well by comparison to almost any other Pharma, and not only that, its profitability is strong—BMY generated a gross margin of 72.5% in Q2 2025, and operating margin of 37.5%, earning $1.8bn of net income, up from $1.3bn in the prior year period.

Furthermore, if we consider BMY's guidance for full-year revenues of $47bn at the midpoint, and EPS of $6.5, that gives us a forward price to sales ("P/S") ratio of 1.9x, and forward price to earnings ("P/E") ratio of ~6.7x, which are both attractively low ratios, and let's not forget the ~6% dividend yield.

With that said, these revenues, profits, and ratios must be sustainable in the long term, and in my view, it is a potential issue that the original architect of BMY's long-term post-Celgene acquisition plans, former CEO Giovanni Caforio, and now its Chief Medical Officer, who had been in the role for more than six years, are no longer overseeing operations.

In summary, we may speculate that the jury is very much out on BMY and its products and pipeline—certainly on Wall Street, given current valuation. Newer drugs, e.g., the cell therapies Abecma and Breyanzi, and psoriasis med Sotyktu, are not yet close to fulfilling the multi-billion dollar revenue targets the company once set for them, and more recently acquired Cobenfy, Krazati, and Camzyos - which represent ~$25bn of M&A activity, are also struggling somewhat.

This helps to explain why BMY's share price is in the doldrums, as does a somewhat unhealthy long-term debt position of $46.2bn, albeit BMY boasts ~$11bn in cash and equivalents, plus $3.9bn of cash flow generated last quarter, and management's stated plan is to "further delever our balance sheet and pay down $10 billion of debt by the first half of 2026 relative to our March 31, 2024, balance."

As a BMY shareholder myself, I'll admit to being somewhat concerned by the current state of play, however I intend to keep holding my shares, not sell them. I am not at this time considering buying more even with the attractive dividend, and a new CMO incoming who has been successful at another Pharma giant.

In many ways, I see parallels with Pfizer Inc. (PFE) here, another stock I own, another Pharma with strong revenues, a strong dividend payout, and a share price that looks far too low by many measures. Given current headwinds in the pharma sector, however—especially the pressure on drug pricing being applied by the Trump administration—I am not about to embark on a Big Pharma buying spree, but I am holding my position in both companies.

The market has been talking for a long time about these two companies' share prices bottoming out, but I have not yet been convinced by that. BMY does have the chance to reignite the potential of its pipeline under a new CMO, however, and I am intrigued to see how this will play out; hence, I am staying the course, and picking up the generous dividend.

data.title: Bristol-Myers Squibb: Growth Is Taking Its Own Time (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Investors weren't impressed with Bristol-Myers Squibb Company's (NYSE:BMY) Q2 2025 results, with the stock down by 5.8% yesterday. There's a good reason for it. Revenues are taking their sweet time to gain momentum as the company's legacy portfolio drags them back with shrinking sales. In the meantime, the company's efforts to drive future growth by locking in promising new treatments are affecting earnings.

Here I discuss that while there are definitely some positives to the revenue picture, they aren't enough to compensate for disappointing profits, even with BMS's positive stock metrics.

Improved revenue performance…

After seeing a 4% YoY drop in revenues ex-FX in Q1 2025, the company did better in Q2 2025. Revenues didn't grow, but they didn't decline either. Reported revenues did better, with an increase of 1% YoY compared with a 6% YoY drop in Q1 2025. Two aspects stand out for the revenue story:

  • Growth portfolio grows past legacy portfolio

Revenues have been held back in the recent past due to the dominance of the legacy portfolio, which covers treatments that are past their patent expiration and are seeing market erosion as a result. But for the first time in Q2 2025, the growth portfolio has exceeded the legacy portfolio, and by a strong 16% (see chart below).

It also saw robust growth of 17% YoY in Q2 2025, sustained from a similar level in Q1 2025. The legacy portfolio, on the other hand, shrank by 13.6% YoY.

  • Cobenfy's contribution grows

The company's treatment for schizophrenia, Cobenfy, merits a mention as well. BMS acquired it as part of the purchase of Karuna Therapeutics, at which time it was undergoing clinical trials. Since the treatment's launch in Q4 2024 in the US, it has seen a 3.5x increase in revenue and a 30% QoQ increase. While the absolute number is still small at $35 million, so far, it is a positive indicator for the future.

…resulting in improved prospects

The company also upgraded its revenue forecast for the full year 2025. At the midpoint of the guidance range, it now expects the figure to be at $47 billion, which is an improvement of 1.5% from the previous guidance. Note that this is the second guidance upgrade for BMS. The revenue figure is now up by 3.3% from the initial projection.

The company isn't entirely out of the woods yet, though, with revenues still slated to contract by 2.7% this year. The extent of expected contraction is smaller than the 5.8% initially envisaged, however. Moreover, with expansion in the company's growth portfolio discussed next, the pullback in revenues could be a thing of the past soon enough.

EPS contracts, guidance reduced

The company's non-GAAP EPS disappointed however. At $1.46, the diluted non-GAAP number was down by 29.5% YoY. The company explains that this is a result of IPRD charges related to its partnership with the German BioNTech SE (BNTX). The company will pay up to $11.1 billion over time to jointly develop and commercialise BioNTech's lung cancer treatment.

Fair as the explanation sounds, it's still disappointing because even if the charge of $0.57 per share is added back to the EPS for Q2 2025, it still shows a 2.8% YoY drop. And this is when the company was working with a favourable base effect. Last year, it drastically reduced its guidance last year along with Q1 2024 results after purchasing Karuna Therapeutics.

After it reported an EPS of $1.8 in Q1 2025 compared with a loss in Q1 2024, it looked like the company was ready for a solid turnaround in earnings. But with the latest acquisition, there are doubts now. Especially as it has slightly reduced its earnings guidance following the results.

At the midpoint of the revised guidance range of $6.35 - $6.65, the EPS is now expected to come in 5.1% lower than indicated by the $6.7 - $7 range earlier. Even if we consider that the projections were raised in Q1 2025, the fact remains that the latest guidance is still 3% lower than the midpoint of the initial range of $6.55 - $6.85.

Stock metrics look good

In contrast with the mixed picture delivered by BMS's latest results, the stock metrics are completely positive, starting with the market multiples. At the midpoint of the current earnings guidance, BMY's forward non-GAAP P/E is at 6.66x, which is far lower than the stock's 5-year average of 18.6x.

Positive as this is, at the present time it's doubtful whether the stock can go back to trading at earlier levels. For context, BMS's five-year average revenue growth is ~9% and diluted EPS growth is 39% compared to the no-growth and shrinking earnings situation it's dealing with right now. That said, the average P/E for five years does indicate how much potential exists in the stock if the company were to get into better financial form.

Next, let's consider the dividends. So far, BMS has paid two quarterly dividends of $0.62 per share each, a 3.3% increase from last year. Assuming that it maintains this level for the rest of 2025, the annual dividend would be $2.48, which translates into a forward dividend yield of 5.73%. This is an improvement from the 5.1% level the last time I checked and is also much higher than the stock's average five-year yield of 3.75%. BMS's forward dividend payout ratio of 38.2% looks alright too.

What next?

An investment in BMY now hinges on how the future of the company evolves. While it has proven itself in the past, at the present time, it's preparing itself for the next stage of growth. And that calls for patience on investors' part.

Signs of a turnaround are already there with the growth portfolio overtaking the legacy portfolio in Q2 2025. It's a relatively new schizophrenia treatment, Cobenfy is also showing good results. The stock's metrics also work in its favour.

The big stumbling block is earnings, though. They have seen a drag since last year, first due to an acquisition and now due to the partnership with BioNTech. While these could well turn out to be highly advantageous investments for BMS, for now they are showing up as reduced profits. And slowing down the stock's momentum.

I'm downgrading BMY to Hold. Better times can be in store for the stock as its revenue growth accelerates and investments pay off. But it could take time.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Palantir: Top 3 Reasons To Buy Before Q2 Earnings
data.description:
Performance Assessment

It looks like nothing leads to even a pause of the Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) train. The stock has outperformed the broader market index, making my last "Market Perform/Hold" update a bit inaccurate:

Thesis

Palantir is set to report its Q2 FY25 earnings on 4 Aug post-market hours. This is my bullish take ahead of the release:

  1. A coincident indicator shows rapid growth in Palantir's broader ecosystem
  2. The recent U.S. Army deal on average adds a significant uptick to US government revenues
  3. Palantir seems to low-ball guidance and outperform result expectations so I expect yet another beat
  4. PLTR's Q2 results need to result in strong earnings upgrades else the valuations may be too hype-driven
  5. PLTR seems to be on the cusp of a breakout.

A coincident indicator show rapid growth in Palantir's broader ecosystem

To get a more real-time assessment of Palantir's growth prospects, I have made it a habit to periodically check for activity in Northslope Technologies, which is a global Palantir partner that provides Foundry & AIP development services to enterprises. The idea is that growth in this Palantir partner would correlate well with the growth of Palantir's ecosystem as a whole, which in turn leads to sustained demand for Palantir's solutions.

It is encouraging to see that Northslope Technologies continues to rapidly grow, with new office openings in London over the past couple of weeks and New York over the last month:

This key Palantir partner seems to be on a hiring spree. So this makes me believe Palantir is likely to post excellent results in Q2 FY25 as well.

The recent U.S. Army deal on average adds a significant uptick to U.S. government revenues

Today, Palantir signed a mega deal worth up to $10 billion with the U.S. Army for up to 10 years to cater to its modern software and requirements for data-driven insights and actions.

The new enterprise agreement - which consolidates 75 contracts (15 prime contracts and 60 related contracts) into a single one - will enhance military readiness, drive operational efficiency, and deliver cost efficiencies.

- U.S. Army Public Affairs.

In the best case scenario, this $10 billion over 10 years would imply an average quarterly revenue run-rate addition of $250 million. This represents a meaningful structural lift of 28% to the latest quarter's $884 million run-rate:

This deal win is likely to set the stage for future success in U.S. government contracts. Indeed, some analysts such as Piper Sandler's Brent Bracelin expect U.S. government revenues to exceed $10 billion by 2030. This compares to $1.3 billion TTM revenues for this segment right now:

Hence, the implied growth in this projection is a CAGR of 45%. Notice how this means the current YoY growth rate in this segment will be sustained.

Palantir seems to low-ball guidance and outperform result expectations, so I expect yet another beat

Palantir has rather consistently delivered revenue guidance and result surprises vs consensus estimates:

So my base case expectation is that this will continue for Q2 FY25 results and Q3 FY25 guidance as well. Assuming median beat levels of 1.28% for guidance and 2.69% for revenues, and taking into account median consensus estimates of $937.20 million and $981.70 million for Q2 and Q3, respectively, I arrive at the following revenue estimates for the next couple of quarters:

On the EBIT margins side too, Palantir has a track record of beats on guidance and results:

But I think this is a less important element vs revenue growth, so I am not baking in much of a beat here. My overall viewpoint on Palantir is not hinged on margin improvement trends.

PLTR's Q2 results needs to result in strong earnings upgrades else the valuations may be too hype-driven

At a 256x 1-yr fwd P/E, Palantir is the most expensive stock compared to various software and technology comparables:

Today, the premium of 555% vs its broader sector's median is near peak levels:

Now, if you're familiar with my previous with my previous coverage on Palantir, you would be familiar with my argument that traditional valuation multiples cannot capture long-term market cap potential such as becoming as big as Microsoft (MSFT). But that does not mean traditional 1-yr fwd P/E valuation multiples should be ignored altogether. I would say that it corresponds to a high risk of a sharp sell-off should the company fail to prompt earnings growth upgrades in the upcoming Q2 results since so far, the earnings index has remained flat whilst the stock's 1-yr fwd P/E multiple has gotten more expensive:

Generally, I think sharp rises in a valuation multiple without a corresponding improvement in the fundamentals outlook indicates overhype in a stock.

PLTR seems to be on the cusp of a breakout

If this is your first time reading a Hunting Alpha article using Technical Analysis, you may want to read this post, which explains how and why I read the charts the way I do. All my charts reflect total shareholder return as they are adjusted for dividends/distributions.

Relative Read of PLTR vs. SP500

On the PLTR vs. SP500 charts, I see a classic bull flag pole followed by a flag pennant and early signs of a breakout to the upside. Hence, I am confident that PLTR will continue outperforming the S&P 500.

Takeaway & Positioning

I am buying some shares of Palantir ahead of the Q2 FY25 earnings release next week as I see strong signs of continued rapid growth in Palantir's broader ecosystem, a meaningful long term CAGR boost to U.S. government revenues and the company's habit of delivering positive surprises on guidance and results almost every quarter. The technical charts also support the bullish perspective too as PLTR vs SP500 is printing a classic bull flag/pennant pattern.

The key risk to the thesis is of course the very high valuations, which gives very little room for error should the stock disappoint the Street by not giving the reasons to justify new earnings growth upgrades. I expect a sharp selloff should Palantir disappoint on results and my plan in that scenario would be to sell off my pre-earnings position add, but still hold on to my larger, long term position in the stock.

How to interpret Hunting Alpha's ratings:

Strong Outperform/Strong Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence. I also have a net long position in the security in my personal portfolio.

Outperform/Buy: Expect the company to outperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Market Perform/Hold: Expect the company to perform in-line with the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Underperform/Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis.

Strong Underperform/Strong Sell: Expect the company to underperform the S&P 500 on a total shareholder return basis, with higher than usual confidence.

The typical time-horizon for my views is multiple quarters to more than a year. It is not set in stone. However, I will share updates on my changes in stance in a pinned comment to this article and may also publish a new article discussing the reasons for the change in view.

data.title: OXLC: Picking Up The Pieces After The CLOpocalypse
data.description:
The popular CLO Equity CEF Oxford Lane Capital Corp (NASDAQ:OXLC) cut its distribution by 11% this week, roiling the sector. The fund's price fell over 12% in the two days following the announcement with a number of other sector funds falling in sympathy.

In this article we discuss some of the dynamics behind this move which will hopefully be useful not only for OXLC shareholders but more generally for CEF investors as well.

Pressure Points

In this section we discuss some of the pressure points that have been building for OXLC income. All of these factors have been clear for some time and, although they can't give us the timing of any distribution shift, they do paint a clear picture of what was likely to happen.

The first key piece of the puzzle is that OXLC had the highest yield in the sector at around 26% on NAV vs. 18.5% average in the rest of the funds (excluding EIC which has a minority allocation to CLO Equity). Even with the cut, however, it still has the highest yield though only barely.

Of course having the highest yield in the sector doesn't necessarily result in a distribution adjustment but if there is an adjustment it's nearly guaranteed to be lower. Moreover, a relatively high distribution rate creates a drag on the NAV which reduces fund assets, leaving fewer assets to service the distribution.

Two, the NAV has been falling over the past year, extending its previous downward trend. Although there is going to be noise in the prices of the underlying assets which will push the NAV around a bit, the downward signal is clear through the monthly noise. Lower NAVs over time means there are fewer assets to support the distribution. It also points to historic overdistribution. Over the past year, the performance of the OXLC NAV is the second worst in the sector, only slightly better than that of ECC.

Three, short-term rates, though still relatively high, have fallen 1% from their peak last year and are expected to keep moving lower. Short-term rates are where they were in late 2022/early 2023 and in that period the fund’s distribution was $0.075 - below the new lower distribution.

The drop in short-term rates resulted in falling net income. By Q1 of this year it had fallen to below the distribution.

Four, the sector has already seen a number of distribution cuts. This includes ECC, EIC and XFLT. On the higher-yielding end of the sector only OXLC and OCCI remained as the funds without recent cuts.

Five, the asset side of the CLO Equity income has been squeezed considerably from the loan repricing / refinancing wave of the last two quarters. Since the start of 2024, the loan market has seen nearly $800bn of loans repriced, about half the total of the entire institutional loan market. This lowers the spread over the base rate collected by CLOs and directly hits the CLO Equity bottom line, putting a big dent in collected income.

After a brief dip in early Q2, loan prices have come back to their previous peaks of early 2025. This points to another refinancing wave, further hitting CLO Equity income.

Six, leverage costs have been rising. The fund retired its 6.75% preferred OXLCM, replacing it with an 8.75% bond and subsequently issued a 7.95% bond, both well above its weighted-average leverage cost. Higher leverage costs squeeze the fund's net income.

Seven, today's market environment is not conducive to strong CLO Equity returns to top up a reduction in income. The best environment for the sector is one where loan prices remain low for an extended period of time. The best years for the sector was the period immediately after the GFC, 2020 and 2022 - all periods of extended market volatility and loan prices that remained subdued for some time. This allows CLO Equity to monetize its reinvestment option, using loan repayments to buy loans well below par. As these loans get repaid at par, the resulting windfall goes straight to CLO Equity coffers. This time around however, loan prices did fall sharply in April but then came right back up nearly as quickly, preventing CLO Equity from collecting significant gains.

Eight, OXLC has the most expensive fee structure in the CLO Equity CEF sector across all three key factors - the base management fee, the incentive fee and the incentive fee hurdle. What this suggests is that, all else equal, its net income production should actually be on the low side in the sector.

All of these factors have weighed on the net income profile of the fund over the recent and longer while. None of them is decisive enough to push the distribution around as that's ultimately management's decision. However, taken together it is not at all surprising that we saw the cut.

Parsing Sentiment

The news of the OXLC distribution cut was received in different ways by the income investor community. In this section we discuss some of the more common sentiments we have come across.

One is that the yield is still high at nearly 23% on NAV and income investors are in it for income. While it's true that today's yield is high, it’s worth checking what the yield is on one’s weighted-average cost basis here because it’s probably much lower. The stock was only lower during COVID meaning the yield on capital allocated to the fund is probably much lower as well.

It’s also important to place the yield in the context of a fund to actually deliver that yield. The reality is that OXLC has not shown the capacity to match its yield in total return terms. Its longer-term total NAV return is in the high single-digits, not in the ~20-25% yield range. This is why the NAV has been falling steadily and why the fund is doing a reverse split. While some investors say they care more about income than total returns, over the longer-term total return is all there is.

Two, there is a view that if one’s cost basis is very low then you’re sort of playing with house money and it’s all good. One’s cost basis i.e. initial investments less distributions received is not a relevant way to analyse returns as it’s pretty much a function of the length of the holding period so investors with zero cost basis may or may not have a strongly-performing asset - it very much depends on the holding period.

Finally, there is a view that it’s dumb to hold a highly leveraged (CLO Equity is leveraged by construction and CLO CEFs themselves are leveraged) credit asset in a weakening macro environment. Again, details matter. Sure, it’s generally not a good idea to 1) pay a premium for credit funds, 2) hold funds with a very high fee structure like OXLC, however leverage is not necessarily dumb - it depends what it costs, what it is on and how it is executed. A fund like EIC has fewer issues of OXLC - it has significantly lower fees and it has a lower-beta portfolio so the forced deleveraging element is less of an issue.

Common vs. Senior Redux

Our loyal readers are no doubt tired of the charts in this section that we revisit time and time again. Specifically, for funds that have both common shares as well as senior securities (i.e. preferreds or bonds), we like to compare the performance across the capital structure to see what investors are taking home.

Looking at the longer-term performance of common vs. preferreds (we go back as far as we can using redeemed securities as well) we see that OXLC preferreds have come out ahead of the common shares to date. They were slightly behind prior to the sell-off this year but not miles behind. The chart also shows clearly the more volatile swings in the common vs. the more stable performance of the preferreds.

If we look at OXLC bonds, the end result here is similar though historically bonds have not kept up with the performance in the common.

The charts above show that to date, the common has not delivered excess return versus the company's senior securities. The charts also show that every time the common underperformed, it typically bounces back quite sharply. It's not clear this will happen this time around given the continued pressure on the fund's income (the Fed is expected to deliver 3-4 rate cuts) however what is clear is that the fund's valuation is unusually attractive. Its discount as of this writing of 15% has not been observed for some time. In short, if there is a time to be tactically bullish on the shares it is now even if from a strategic perspective OXLC remains a tough hold.

Stance and Takeaways

We have not held OXLC in our Income Portfolios. The combination of typically high premium, high fees and instances of forced deleveraging haven't made the common shares very attractive. We are, however, big fans of the bonds. There are 4 bonds in the suite, 3 of which feature yield north of 8%. This may not seem like much in the context of the 27% OXLC common share yield however recall that the fund's longer-term total NAV return is not actually much above 8%.

For now we are constructive on the common shares given the rare double-digit discount. However, this is more of a tactical rather than strategic view. Our preference in the sector remains with funds like EIC and XFLT. They have modest fee structures by CLO CEF standards, creating a smaller drag on the NAV and passing on more of the fund's income to shareholders. For defensive investors, we would stick with the CLO CEF bonds from either OXLC or ECC.

They also have a mixed allocation with CLO Equity at 25% and 38% respectively. This keeps the chance of a forced deleveraging under control. Their discounts are both at 10%.

data.title: Palantir: A $10 Billion Upside (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
My bull case on Palantir Technologies Inc. (NASDAQ:PLTR) was traditionally based on the commercial segment, particularly in the U.S. However, with the recent enterprise agreement with the U.S. Army, worth up to $10 billion for the next ten years, I am revisiting my thesis going into Q2 2025 earnings on August 4th.

While I still remain bullish on this stock (remember, Palantir redefined the Rule of 40, pushing it up to be the Rule of 80), I believe the government segment will be predominant in terms of revenue contribution and growth for the next few years. The downside of the commercial segment is the lack of growth outside of the U.S. This was particularly noticeable in the last quarter, when non-U.S. commercial revenue declined 5% YOY and 11% sequentially. On the other hand, non-U.S. government revenue was up by 45% YOY and 2% sequentially. Essentially, the non-U.S. government segment is growing at relatively the same pace as the U.S. government unit (45% YOY and 9% sequentially in the last quarter). Therefore, I expect the growth gap between government and commercial to persist.

I have now downgraded my rating to a Buy going into Q2 earnings, as I believe the massive deal with the U.S. Army will pressure (to a certain extent) margins. This is not great news for the FY 2025 operating income guidance ($1.711 billion — $1.723 billion), as a surprise to the downside could hurt the stock, similarly to what happened with Amazon.com, Inc. (AMZN) after its operating income guidance disappointed investors.

Why Commercial Is Behind Government

My bull case always leaned on the commercial segment, rather than the government one. That recently changed. Here's why.

First, I always argued that commercial has more long-term upside than the government from a growth perspective. The AIP platform can essentially be integrated by any corporation around the world, looking to right-size their headcount. I already discussed in a previous coverage why I believe agents will replace a significant number of jobs, particularly white collar ones.

So, the potential of the AIP platform is there. However, the adoption is quite anemic and doesn't quite live up to the hype, particularly outside of the U.S. Here is what CEO Alex Karp said in Q4 last year (emphasis added):

We see as much as I personally care about the broader West, including Continental Europe, despite our best efforts and working on every day, it's anemic. We're growing around 4% on 13% of our company.

He further emphasized in Q1 (emphasis added):

If you take the 90% of our business that is outside of Europe, which is going through very structural change and doesn't quite get AI, maybe in the near future will get AI, we are hoping and praying,

Karp doesn't have much hope in Europe (from a commercial perspective), and I confirm his disbelief, as I work for a large aerospace corporation on that continent that is not keen on AI. This drag is clearly noticeable in the Q1 earnings results. Non-U.S. commercial revenue declined 5% YOY and 11% sequentially, totaling $141 million.

As for U.S. commercial, there is indeed explosive growth (71% YOY in the past quarter), but the run rate of just $1 billion is still small compared to the upside that the AIP platform provides to enterprises. Furthermore, the backlog (Palantir calls it "TCV, total contract value") for U.S. commercial is just $810 million. Yes, that's 2.4x more than last year; however, the absolute value (i.e., $810 million) doesn't quite justify the premium at which the company is currently trading (P/E of 687).

Looking at the guidance for FY 2025, management expects the US commercial segment to grow by 68% YOY, with a midpoint revenue guidance of $ 1.178 billion. Again, great growth figures, but the absolute value ($1.18 billion) doesn't quite justify the valuation premium.

My next point refers to margins. Management is kind enough to share with us the contribution of each segment (in other words, the profitability) in their financial reporting.

As seen above, the margins of the commercial and the government segments are pretty much the same (61% and 62%, respectively). This is not what one would initially expect, as government contracts tend to last longer, and therefore, demand a volume-based discounted price. Back in February, I was quite certain that these discounts would further deepen after Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth ordered the Pentagon agencies and the military to cut down 8% of their spending, each year, for the next 5 years. That's $50 billion per year in savings.

So, I initially projected that margins would decline as the company reached a plateau in defense contracts. I was dead wrong.

Momentum In the Government Segment

Recently, the U.S. Army awarded Palantir a massive contract worth up to $10 billion over a period of 10 years. According to the press release (emphasis added):

The EA [Enterprise Agreement] will enhance military readiness and drive operational efficiency while delivering significant cost efficiencies.

Furthermore, the deal consolidates 75 contracts into a single one (emphasis added),

"accelerating the delivery of proven commercial software to warfighters while removing contract and re-seller pass-through fees."

Based on the words that I emphasized above, it appears that the army is looking to cut down costs with this contract, which is aligned with Hegseth's 8% cost-cutting goal that I mentioned in the previous section. To me, this massive $10 billion contract is the ultimate confirmation that Palantir's Foundry, Gotham, and Maven Smart System platforms are truly effective from a cost perspective. As you may already know, both Foundry and Gotham are using a single codebase, which means the Army doesn't pay for duplicate software licenses or parallel integration projects for each mission domain. Also, through Apollo’s license metering model, the Army only pays for active user seats and compute nodes.

This $10 billion deal comes on top of the Maven Smart System contract, worth $795 million, after an expansion was announced in May this year. I believe that in the next earnings call, we will get more information on whether the $10 billion contract consolidates the smaller Maven deal or whether they are separate from each other.

Other big contracts include the provision of the Maven Smart System to NATO for use across all 32 member states. This is great news for the non-U.S. government segment, which is growing at 45% YOY, unlike the recent decline in the non-U.S. commercial. So far, the growth seems to be driven by UK healthcare and defense contracts, along with the new Maven partnership with NATO.

In the U.S., aside from the massive $10 billion contract with the U.S. Army, the TITAN program has been a growth driver for Palantir, with the first TITAN vehicle delivered in Q1 2025. The current MSS (Maven Smart System) contract that I mentioned earlier runs through May 2029 and has a total contract ceiling of $1.275 billion ($480M + $795M).

Overall, I now see more upside in the government segment than in commercial, looking at a 1-2 year timeframe. Therefore, I expect the growth gap between Palantir's commercial and government segments to persist.

Risks

I believe the biggest risk to consider going into Q2 earnings comes from a margin perspective. There is something in the Army's press release that I didn't quite like:

The agreement establishes volume-based discounts for the contract’s performance period of up to 10 years.

Considering the timeframe of 10 years, those volume-based discounts could be hefty. While these contracts will support the revenue growth, currently in the mid-40s for the government segment, they will most likely pressure profitability.

So far, the margin for the government segment is quite impressive, sitting at 62% in Q1. However, the presence of low-margin revenue could depress that metric.

From a guidance perspective, the FY 2025 expectation for adj. operating income is between $1.711 billion — $1.723 billion. Below is the Street's expectation for this metric:

I see a risk of management being conservative from a profitability perspective due to the $10 contract with the Army. If their FY 2025 guidance for operating income disappoints the Street, we could be in for an unpleasant selloff, similar to what we have seen this week with Amazon.

Looking Ahead

Q2 earnings are right around the corner, with results coming out on August 4 after market close. I have no doubts that the main topic will be the $10 billion enterprise agreement contract with the U.S. Army. Therefore, I recommend keeping a close eye on any comments on how quickly they will be able to materialize the potential $10 billion into sales. At the end of Q1, Palantir's backlog was $5.97 billion, up 45% YOY and 10% sequentially. Even though the $10 billion contract announcement falls within Q3, analysts will most likely ask management to comment on how quickly the deal can flow into the income statement.

As for the guidance, I included below the expectations for Q2 and FY 2025.

I have a high conviction that the revenue guidance for FY 2025 will be upgraded, considering the recent deal with the Army. However, I cannot say the same for the US commercial segment.

As for the Street expectations for Q2 and Q3 this year, the EPS consensus is the same for both quarters, at 0.14.

In regard to revenue, the Street is expecting growth to be in the mid to high 30s.

Overall, I believe the company will beat these expectations, although I warn that the operating income guidance for FY 2025 could disappoint investors if the volume contracts within the $10 billion deal with the Army end up pressuring margins.
